diagrams.title: Five-Stage Instruction Cycle
diagrams.description: A linear flow diagram showing the five sequential stages of instruction execution: Fetch (F), Decode (D), Execute (E), Memory Access (M), and Write Back (W). Each stage is represented as a labeled box or segment connected by arrows to show the flow of a single instruction through the processor.
diagrams.purpose: Explains the fundamental instruction cycle phases that every CPU performs for each instruction, forming the basis for understanding pipelining.

diagrams.title: Classic RISC Pipeline (5-Stage)
diagrams.description: A Gantt-chart style timing diagram showing multiple instructions (I1, I2, I3, I4, I5) executing simultaneously in different pipeline stages. The diagram uses horizontal rows for each instruction and vertical columns for clock cycles, with colored blocks filling the grid to show overlapping execution. Each instruction moves one stage to the right each clock cycle.
elements: Pipeline stage rows (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB)
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates how pipelining achieves instruction-level parallelism by allowing multiple instructions to be in different stages simultaneously, dramatically increasing throughput.

diagrams.title: Non-Pipelined vs Pipelined Execution Comparison
diagrams.description: A side-by-side comparison showing two timelines: top half shows sequential non-pipelined execution of 5 instructions taking 25 clock cycles (5 stages × 5 instructions), bottom half shows pipelined execution completing 5 instructions in only 9 clock cycles. Arrows or brackets highlight the time difference and speedup ratio.
diagrams.purpose: Visually proves the performance benefit of pipelining by comparing completion times, showing how the pipeline fills and drains to achieve nearly N× speedup for N stages.
